petitioner_background: The petitioner is a chemist seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in the sciences under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act, specifically in the field of organometallic chemistry. At the time of filing on June 23, 2003, he was working in the Department of Chemistry at Ohio State University. His career includes research on heteropoly metallic complexes at Lanzhou University (1985-88), work at Henan College of Education (1988-2000) where he rose from Lecturer to Associate Professor to Professor, and a research position at the prestigious Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (1996-1999), where he conducted seminal research on butterfly-type clusters, diyne-coordinated clusters, alkyne and alkynyl complexes, and asymmetric complexes for chiral pharmaceutical catalysis.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence showing that he served in an editorial capacity and provided peer review services for a variety of scientific publications.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that the evidence of serving in an editorial capacity and providing peer review services for scientific publications was adequate to satisfy this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted several letters of support from prominent scientists including researchers at the Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics (Chinese Academy of Sciences), the Laboratory of Surface Organometallic Chemistry at the National Center of Scientific Research in Lyon, France, an Endowed Chair in the Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry at the University of Notre Dame, Zhengzhou University in China, and the Bavarian Academy of Sciences/University of Munich. Additional letters came from researchers at MIT, University of Missouri-St. Louis, Northern Illinois University, and UOP (a company specializing in catalysts and process technology). The letters described his discovery of the regioselectivity of metal fragment exchange reactions in linked tetrahedral cluster complexes (considered a breakthrough for constructing higher nuclearity hetero-nuclear clusters), his synthesis and characterization of chiral tetrahedral cluster complexes, his work showing a two coordination mode in linked RuCo3(CO) clusters in the solid state, his original work on zirconium and lanthanide organohydroborate complexes with significance for high-performance olefin polymerization catalysts, his contributions to bimetallic systems combining transition metals and lanthanides, his work activating C-H bonds by zirconium complexes useful in organic synthesis, and his catalytic conversion of propyl alcohol to propylene. The petitioner also submitted evidence indicating an aggregate of more than 100 citations to his published work.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO noted that publication by itself is not a strong indication of impact, but that citations provide persuasive and credible evidence of outside reaction to the petitioner's work. The unusually large number of more than 100 citations bolstered the witnesses' claims that the petitioner's findings are of major significance in the organometallic chemistry field. The AAO found that the record adequately demonstrated the petitioner's contributions were important not only to the research institutions where he worked but throughout the greater field, with leading scientists from around the world acknowledging the value of his work and its major significance to the scientific community. The AAO concluded the evidence satisfied this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of his authorship of articles appearing in publications such as Journal of the American Chemical Society, Organometallics, Inorganic Chemistry, Journal of Chemical Research, and Journal of Organometallic Chemistry. He also submitted copies of numerous articles that cite his work. A second letter from a witness stated that the petitioner had published more than 40 papers in top-rated international journals and that his research had been cited more than 100 times over a span of only the last five years (1999-2004), described as an amazingly high citation rate, much better than many independent researchers established in the area.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO observed that when judging influence and impact of published work, the act of publication is not as reliable a gauge as citation history. Publication alone may serve as evidence of originality, but it is difficult to conclude a published article is important or influential without evidence that other researchers have relied upon the findings. In this case, the unusually large number of citations to the petitioner's articles demonstrated widespread interest in and reliance on his work. The AAO found the evidence adequate to satisfy this criterion.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO sustained the appeal and approved the petition, finding that the petitioner satisfied three of the regulatory criteria required for classification as an alien of extraordinary ability. While not all of the evidence carried the weight imputed to it by the petitioner, the totality of the evidence established an overall pattern of sustained national and international acclaim and extraordinary ability in the organometallic chemistry field. The petitioner also established that he seeks to continue working in the same field in the United States and that his entry would substantially benefit the United States prospectively. The petitioner overcame the stated grounds for denial and established eligibility for the benefits sought under section 203 of the Act.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
